disease.name: Hepatitis D (Delta Virus)
disease.note: HDV (delta virus): defective RNA virus requiring HBV surface antigen (HBsAg) for entry and assembly. Most severe form of viral hepatitis; accelerates cirrhosis and HCC. Highest prevalence in Amazon basin, Mediterranean, Middle East, Central Africa.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Superinfection -> HIGH risk of fulminant/chronic; Coinfection -> moderate risk; Fulminant hepatic failure -> EMERGENCY ICU

subtypes.name: HDV-HBV Coinfection
subtypes.note: Simultaneous HDV and HBV acquisition. Often self-limited (similar to acute HBV). Risk of fulminant hepatitis ~5%.

subtypes.name: HDV Superinfection
subtypes.note: HDV acquired in chronic HBV carrier. Often severe, leads to chronic HDV in 70-80%. Accelerated progression to cirrhosis and HCC.

emergency.action: Admit to ICU if fulminant hepatic failure (encephalopathy, coagulopathy)
IV fluids and supportive care for acute severe hepatitis
Evaluate for liver transplant in decompensated liver failure

inpatient.action: Pegylated interferon alpha 180 mcg/week for 48 weeks for chronic HDV (responders)
Monitor LFTs, HDV RNA, HBV DNA, INR closely
N-acetylcysteine IV for fulminant hepatitis (limited evidence)

outpatient.action: Pegylated interferon alpha continuation if responding
HCC surveillance: US + AFP q6m in chronic HDV with cirrhosis
Monitor HDV RNA q3-6m during treatment

refractory.action: Liver transplant evaluation for decompensated cirrhosis
Clinical trials for novel antivirals (bulevirtide, lonafarnib, REP 2139)

preventive.action: HBV vaccination (prevents HDV by preventing HBV)
Avoid sharing needles, safe sex practices

disease.pathophysiology: HDV enters hepatocytes via HBsAg attachment to NTCP receptor. Direct cytopathic effect of HDV (delta antigen inhibits host RNA polymerase) combined with immune-mediated CD8+ T-cell injury. In coinfection, both HBV and HDV resolve simultaneously. In superinfection, HDV outcompetes HBV leading to suppression of HBV replication but severe HDV-driven liver injury. Accelerated fibrogenesis through stellate cell activation.

diagnosticClues.clue: HBsAg positive patient with severe acute hepatitis or acute-on-chronic deterioration
Rapidly progressive course compared to typical HBV
Low or undetectable HBV DNA with severe hepatitis in HBsAg carrier

exclusionClues.clue: Isolated HBV infection with expected course
